title: Reading and Use of English - Part 5
description: You are going to read a magazine article about an intellectual process known as critical thinking. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.

content.title: The Silent Architect: How Buildings Shape Our Minds
content.subtitle: Understanding the profound psychological impact of the built environment.
content.text: Winston Churchill once remarked, 'We shape our buildings; thereafter, they shape us.' While this may sound like a poetic flourish, modern environmental psychology has proven it to be a literal truth. [31] For most of human history, we lived in environments characterized by organic shapes and natural light, but the rapid urbanization of the last two centuries has forced us into 'geometric cages' that our brains are not yet fully evolved to process. [/31] The cold, right-angled efficiency of modernism often ignores the fundamental biological needs of the human animal. || [32] One of the most striking findings in this field is the concept of 'biophilia'—our innate tendency to seek connections with nature and other forms of life. [/32] Studies have shown that hospital patients in rooms with views of trees recover significantly faster and require less pain medication than those staring at a brick wall. Similarly, offices with natural light and plants see a measurable boost in employee productivity and a decrease in absenteeism. It seems that when we are stripped of nature, our stress levels rise, even if we are not consciously aware of the cause. || [33] Furthermore, the scale of a building can profoundly affect our sense of social agency. [/33] Massive, monolithic structures like those found in 'Brutalist' architecture can make individuals feel insignificant and powerless. In contrast, 'human-scale' design—which prioritizes walkable streets, varied facades, and accessible public spaces—fosters a sense of community and belonging. A city of skyscrapers can be a city of lonely people if there is nowhere for them to meet at eye level. || [34] The layout of interior spaces is equally influential. The 'open-plan' office, once touted as a revolution in collaboration, has been revealed as a psychological disaster for many. [/34] Without physical boundaries, the brain is constantly on high alert, scanning for social threats and distractions. The result is 'noise pollution' and a loss of the 'deep work' capacity that is essential for complex tasks. True innovation, it turns out, often requires the ability to withdraw into a private cocoon. || [35] However, the solution is not to return to a pre-industrial past, but to embrace 'evidence-based design.' [/35] This involves using data from neuroscience and psychology to create environments that actively promote well-being. From the color of the walls to the acoustics of a classroom, every detail can be optimized to reduce cognitive load and enhance emotional health. Architecture is moving from an art of the ego to a science of the soul. || [36] Ultimately, as we face a future of increasing density and climate instability, the way we build will determine the quality of our collective mental health. [/36] We can no longer afford to treat buildings as mere 'machines for living.' They are the vessels of our consciousness, and if we continue to ignore their psychological impact, we risk building a world that is efficient on paper but uninhabitable for the human spirit.

questions.question: In the first paragraph, the author suggests that modern urban environments...
options: A) have helped humans overcome their biological limitations.
B) have been designed to maximize organic shapes and natural light.
C) conflict with the evolutionary development of the human mind.
D) are more suited to the human brain than traditional rural settings.
questions.explanation: The text states urbanization forced us into 'geometric cages' that our brains are 'not yet fully evolved to process.'

questions.question: The concept of 'biophilia' is used to explain why...
options: A) being in contact with nature has measurable health and work benefits.
B) patients in hospitals should be allowed to spend more time outdoors.
C) brick walls are the most stressful type of architectural material.
D) humans are naturally afraid of living in high-rise buildings.
questions.explanation: The text links biophilia to faster recovery in hospitals with views of trees and increased productivity in offices with plants and natural light.

questions.question: How does the 'scale' of architecture affect people, according to the author?
options: A) Brutalist architecture encourages people to work harder and be more efficient.
B) Massive structures can diminish an individual's sense of importance.
C) Skyscrapers are the best way to foster a sense of community in big cities.
D) Larger buildings always lead to better social outcomes than smaller ones.
questions.explanation: The author says monolithic structures can make individuals feel 'insignificant and powerless,' contrasting them with 'human-scale' design.

questions.question: What is the author's critique of the 'open-plan' office?
options: A) it creates an environment that prevents concentrated, high-level thinking.
B) It encourages too much collaboration, which leads to slower results.
C) It is too expensive to maintain compared to traditional offices.
D) It makes it impossible for employees to scan for social threats.
questions.explanation: The text states it leads to a 'loss of the deep work capacity' needed for complex tasks because the brain is constantly distracted.

questions.question: According to the author, the future of architecture lies in...
options: A) focusing purely on the aesthetic preferences of individual architects.
B) rejecting all industrial materials in favor of natural ones.
C) reducing the cost of construction by simplifying building designs.
D) using scientific research to improve the impact of buildings on people.
questions.explanation: The text mentions 'evidence-based design,' which uses 'data from neuroscience and psychology' to promote well-being.

questions.question: What is the author's final warning in the conclusion?
options: A) Future buildings must be more efficient to survive climate instability.
B) The human spirit is strong enough to survive in any type of building.
C) Machines will soon be better at designing habitable environments than humans.
D) Designing buildings without considering psychology could lead to widespread unhappiness.
questions.explanation: The author warns that if we ignore psychological impact, we risk a world that is 'uninhabitable for the human spirit,' affecting 'collective mental health.'
